data.title: Wasatch Emerging Markets Small Cap Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

Following a sharp initial selloff tied to global trade tensions, stocks of small companies in developing nations rebounded to deliver strong second-quarter returns. The benchmark MSCI Emerging Markets Small Cap Index finished 17.17% higher, boosted by outsized gains in Korea’s tech-heavy stock market. The Wasatch Emerging Markets Small Cap Fund—Investor Class returned 13.78% for the quarter, trailing the benchmark.

The period began on a weak note, but sentiment improved after the Trump administration put new reciprocal tariffs on hold for 90 days. That shift enabled the Index to recapture lost ground by early May, with gains continuing through the quarter’s end. Korea led all Index constituents during the period, making the Fund’s underweight there the largest source of underperformance versus the benchmark. Comparative results were also held back by a global information-technology (IT) holding and by stock selection in Brazil and China.

While the Fund’s India stocks posted a solid, double-digit return in the quarter, they lagged the benchmark’s Indian positions and hindered relative performance. Still, we were encouraged to see our most heavily weighted emerging market deliver positive results following two quarters of declines. Renewed investor optimism in India followed stronger-than-expected GDP growth, which accelerated to 7.4% in the March quarter. Easing inflation pressures allowed the Reserve Bank of India, the country’s central bank, to lower interest rates, which fueled investment activity. In addition, India’s limited reliance on exports helped insulate its economy from global trade tensions.

Stock selection in Taiwan and the Fund’s holding in the Baltic region were the biggest sources of strength relative to the benchmark. The Fund also benefited from underweight positioning in Turkey, Saudi Arabia and Thailand—the Index’s three weakest-performing components.

Details of the quarter

The strongest contributor to Fund performance for the quarter was ASPEED Technology, Inc. The Taiwan-based fabless chip designer reported 104% sales growth and a 126% year-over-year surge in earnings per share for its most-recent quarter, supported by margin expansion and tighter cost controls. The company’s management also indicated a potential recovery in market share previously lost because of differing product upgrade cycles relative to competitors.

Baltic Classifieds Group PLC was also a top contributor. Headquartered in Lithuania, the company operates online classifieds platforms for automobiles, real estate, jobs and general merchandise. Baltic Classifieds’ dominant market position, high margins and cash-generative business model continue to reinforce our long-term investment thesis. The stock trended steadily higher during the quarter amid optimism that the Trump administration might broker a peace agreement between Russia and Ukraine, which could potentially ease geopolitical tensions in the Baltic region.

Another strong stock in the Fund was AU Small Finance Bank Ltd. The company primarily serves unbanked and underbanked low- and middle-income borrowers in India. After-tax profit rose 18% year-over-year in AU’s most recent quarter, driven by robust core income growth and improved operational efficiency. Positive takeaways from our meeting with AU’s CEO included signs of progress in the company’s application for a universal bank license. If granted by the Reserve Bank of India, the new license would allow AU to expand both its operations and service offerings.

The greatest detractor from Fund performance for the quarter was Globant SA (GLOB) as the global IT consulting firm continued to navigate a challenging operating environment. Globant’s management had lowered guidance earlier in the year and then cut its full-year forecast again in May following quarterly revenue and earnings that fell short of estimates. Despite these near-term headwinds, we believe Globant remains aligned with long-term digital transformation trends, including the rising adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) and automation. With many corporate clients still in the early stages of integrating these technologies, we view the company as a likely long-term beneficiary.

Raia Drogasil SA (OTCPK:RADLY) was also a significant detractor, and we sold our position on deteriorating fundamentals. The company operates Brazil’s leading chain of drugstores. Raia Drogasil has been contending with high inflation, which has driven up costs while limiting its ability to use price increases to boost profitability. With the company’s latest quarterly results missing estimates and Brazil’s economic environment still uncertain, we decided to move on.

Another weak stock in the Fund was Aditya Vision Ltd. The company is a leading electronics retailer serving rural and semiurban areas of India. Aditya continued to post strong revenue gains, but investors reacted negatively as costs associated with rapid store expansion pressured margins and earnings. We remain patient and view the impact on profitability as a natural consequence of the company’s investment in future growth. ( Current and future holdings are subject to change and risk.)

Outlook and Positioning

As we look ahead to the second half of the year, the market backdrop remains somewhat less predictable, with renewed geopolitical uncertainty adding to the mix. Uncertainties around trade policy pose another major wildcard.

Reflecting our continued emphasis on in-depth, on-the-ground research, we’ve stepped up our travel to Taiwan and India—the Fund’s two most heavily weighted geographies. We recently completed our second trip to Taiwan this year, with our efforts focused on rapid developments in AI. We avoided chasing stocks during the island’s AI-driven rally, believing that the real-world application of these technologies remains in its early stages—and that much of the runup in so-called “AI stocks” was speculative rather than rooted in fundamentals.

With the help of two new senior hires, we’re mapping the entire value chain surrounding AI and advanced semiconductors in Taiwan—everything from test pins to cooling systems and cabling for AI servers. We’re seeking companies whose growth is powered by AI, advanced computing and the surge in capital spending that has accompanied this wave of innovation. Our objective is to identify reasonably valued businesses positioned to benefit from durable trends and capable of delivering strong revenue and earnings growth over time.

During our recent two-week trip to India—our second of three planned for this year—we visited a wide range of companies across Bangalore, Coimbatore, Chennai, Delhi and Jaipur. The itinerary included both existing holdings and prospective investments.

Meetings with current portfolio companies helped clarify key questions and generally reinforced our investment theses. We also met with firms under consideration for purchase across sectors such as IT, industrials, financials and health care. These conversations uncovered a broad set of high-quality, reasonably valued businesses for potential inclusion in the Fund. Two of these companies, along with one identified during our Taiwan trip, have already been added. Following those visits, we’re continuing to broaden the scope of our research. A trip is planned for July to several smaller emerging markets in the Middle East and Europe, with stops in Lithuania, Poland, Morocco and Greece.

Evaluating management teams remains a cornerstone of our investment process. We believe our face-to-face meetings help us distinguish good management teams from truly exceptional ones—a distinction we view as critical to delivering long-term results.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely,

Scott Thomas, Dan Chace, Ajay Krishnan, Kevin Unger and Anh Hoang

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. Current performance may be lower or higher than the data quoted. For the most recent month-end performance data, visit wasatchglobal.com. The Advisor may absorb certain expenses, without which total returns would have been lower. Wasatch Funds will deduct a 2% redemption fee on Fund shares held 60 days or less. data.title: Exelon: Smart Bet For Yield And Surging Demand
data.description:
Growth stocks in the AI space like NVIDIA (NVDA) are all the rage, and are priced accordingly. But what about the ‘pick and shovel’ style plays that enable AI to grow in size and popularity? I would consider select utilities as falling squarely in the enabler bucket, and many trade at very reasonable valuations while sporting solid long-term growth potential.

This brings me to Exelon Corporation (NASDAQ:EXC), which I last covered in June, highlighting its attractive mix of income and growth, and its surging electricity demand from data centers. It appears the market has also agreed with my thesis, with the stock rising by 6.5% since my last piece, sitting ahead of the 5.5% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe.

Over the past 12 months, EXC has risen by 17%, and yet its PE remains reasonable at under 17x and it has a 3.5% yield. In this article, I revisit EXC including recent quarterly earnings and outlook, and discuss why its is reasonably priced for growth and income investors, so let’s get started!

Why EXC?

Exelon Corporation is one of the largest utilities in the U.S., serving 10 million customers across six states across the Midwest, Northeast, and Mid-Atlantic regions. It has a fully regulated business model that’s focused on transmission and distribution infrastructure, grid modernization, and clean energy transition initiatives. EXC has a strong track record of reliability, with four of its utilities ranking in the top eight most reliable in the U.S.

EXC recently demonstrated solid Q2 2025 earnings of $0.39 per share, sitting ahead of management’s expectations from Q1. While this is down from the $0.47 achieved in the prior year period, this was due to higher storm-related costs including a $50 million customer relief fund and storms in early summer.

Notably, EXC’s PECO utility (in Pennsylvania) was hit with one of the largest storms in recent history, with peak customer outages surpassing 325,000. Offsetting the one-time headwinds were higher distribution rates at PECO and higher distribution and transmission rates at PHI, as shown below.

Management reaffirmed full year adjusted EPS guidance of $2.69 at the midpoint of range, which represents a robust 7.6% growth rate from $2.50 achieved in the prior year. Notably, Q3 is expected to contribute to 29% of full-year earnings assuming normalized storm activity and weather patterns. Management is guiding for 5% to 7% annual EPS growth through 2028, driven by rate base growth at a 7.4% CAGR and investment opportunities.

Speaking of which, EXC is well-positioned to capitalize on its regulatory and operational strength to lead grid transformation. This includes executing on a $38 billion capital investment plan through 2028 with an additional $10-15 billion of transmission investment opportunities beyond that.

EXC’s transmission investment estimates have risen in response to a surge in demand stemming from data processing needs. This includes loads on the utility coming from AI, cloud, and quantum computing. Management is seeing strong data center activity across multiple jurisdictions, including its Top 5 data market state of Illinois, as noted during the Q&A session of the recent earnings call:

Q: And then wondering if you could elaborate on how data center discussions have been progressing recently. How quickly do you anticipate firming up more megawatts and kind of any reactions that you're seeing from the industry to some of the large load tariffs that you're rolling out?

A: We talked about we see significant activity in Illinois. And we talked about the second cluster study that's being presented and started in August, which will provide more clarity as we go forward. And then let me just be very clear, we're also seeing data center activity across our other jurisdiction. Pennsylvania, Maryland. So this is not just a one state. You just see Illinois being in the top 5 of what we're doing. So understand it's driving forward, and you'll see more of those announcements coming in. But doing it the right way with reliability in mind is key.

Meanwhile, EXC carries a solid A- credit rating from S&P. This is supported by a safe FFO-to-Debt ratio of 14%, which sits 200 basis points higher than the Moody’s downgrade threshold of 12%. Importantly for income investors, EXC currently yields a respectable 3.5% and the dividend is well-covered by a 59% payout ratio. The dividend also comes with an impressive 5-year CAGR of 8%.

Lastly, I continue to see long-term value in EXC at the current price of $45.54 with a forward PE of 16.9. While this sits above its historical PE of 14.4, I believe the premium is justified considering EXC’s growth catalysts stemming from power-hungry data center demand.

Admittedly, some of the near term alpha has been realized after the rise in price since my last piece. However, I believe EXC remains reasonably priced considering its forward growth prospects. EXC could well-deliver market-level total returns, when factoring in the 3.5% dividend yield and 5-7% annual EPS growth potential. This could come with less volatility than the market average due to the steady utility earnings and with a far higher yield than that of the S&P 500 at the same time.

Risks to EXC include different regulatory climates across its jurisdictions, which could result in different rate case rulings. Unfavorable rulings could slow EXC’s earnings growth. Plus, some states like Illinois and Maryland are pushing for renewable mandates, which could require costly grid upgrades. Lastly, unexpected weather events could result in service disruptions and unexpected repair costs.

Investor Takeaway

Exelon Corporation offers an attractive mix of income, growth, and defensive stability in a volatile market. This makes it a standout “pick and shovel” play in the AI-driven data center boom. While it’s not a flashy tech stock, EXC is powering the digital future behind the scenes, with rising electricity demand driving major grid investments and regulatory support across key markets.

EXC remains attractively valued for long-term investors seeking potential reliable returns with a solid 3.5% yield, and a forward PE of under 17. The recent run-up has priced in some near-term upside, but the long-term growth story remains firmly intact. As such, I view EXC as being reasonably priced for growth and continue to rate it a ‘Buy’.

data.title: Honda: Eyes On Results Miss And Re-Rating Potential
data.description:
I'm assigning a Hold rating to Honda Motor Co., Ltd. (NYSE:HMC). My opinion of HMC is mixed, considering its poor outlook and undemanding valuations.

Company Description

Japan's HMC calls itself the "worldʼs leading manufacturer of motorcycles" in its corporate disclosures.

It sold 21M motorcycles in FY25 (YE Mar 31, 2025), which represented two-fifths of worldwide industry volumes. This is the firm's core business accounting for 55% and 17% of its recent fiscal year operating income and revenues, respectively.

The other major division is automobile that contributed two-thirds of topline and one-fifth of EBIT in FY2025.

The numbers suggest that autos have weaker margins than motorbikes for the company. HMC's remaining sales are derived from financial services and power products.

The Group's Top Line Split By Geography

Both Results And Outlook Were Below Expectations

Earlier this week, HMC issued a 6-K filing revealing its 1QFY26 financials.

Its "Operating Income" or "OI" dropped 50% YoY to ¥0.24T in the initial three months of the new fiscal year. This was 21% lower than the consensus projection as per S&P Capital IQ data.

The company's latest quarterly bottomline amounting to JPY 197B was half of what it was for 1QFY2025. That also fell short of the sell-side's predictions by 10%.

In the preceding section, I highlighted the motorcycles unit's market leadership and the automotive operations' relatively inferior profitability. The divergence in the performances of these two businesses was responsible for its worse-than-anticipated numbers in the Apr-Jun (calendar year) time frame.

The motorbike division's 1QFY2026 OI rose 6% to JPY 0.19T on a year-on-year basis. The outperformer was the Brazilian sub-segment which witnessed sales increases and price hikes (actual figures undisclosed). The positive growth momentum for this market should be sustainable.

HMC mentioned at the Q1 analyst briefing (source: S&P Capital IQ transcript) that "demand is higher than the supply" and "pricing is healthy" in Brazil. It aims to raise the maximum manufacturing throughput there by +23% in unit terms over time.

But the firm's automobiles business recorded a -¥30 billion operating loss last quarter.

It highlighted a 1Q charge of -JPY 0.11T pertaining to "write-offs of development assets for EV models" and "provisions for losses on (U.S. market) EVs (Electric Vehicles)" in the earnings slides.

Almost three-fifth of HMC's revenue comes from the North American region. It's also in the process of transitioning from "ICEs (Internal Combustion Engines)" to "EVs" like many other traditional automakers.

Tariff- and EV-related expenses are likely to remain a drag on its short-term profits. The group's full-year OI guide is ¥0.7 trillion. It translates into a 42 percent YoY decline, and a 22% miss compared to the prior analysts' estimate.

My take is that HMC is undeserving of being a Buy-rated name. Investors would be wary of its unfavorable prospects for the near future.

But Valuations Are Depressed With Possible Mid-Term Re-Rating Triggers

I think it isn't right to be a bear on this name. The market pricing of HMC's shares is reflective of its challenges to a huge extent.

Honda boasts a P/B of 0.54 times (source: S&P Capital IQ). Its Japanese peer Toyota Motor (TM) (7203.T) is trading at near to book value. This is fair for now in my opinion. The latter's consensus FY26 "Return On Equity" or "ROE" forecast of 8.1 percent is way higher than the former's 4.9%.

It is taking actions to close the valuation gap.

The group has a "lazy balance sheet", which has hurt its financial returns metrics like ROEs. Its current net cash-to-market cap of 47% is still elevated, even though this is better than the 61% a year ago.

In December '24, the firm initiated a ¥1.1T repurchase program. It's encouraging to learn that 85% of this plan was already done by end-Jul.

HMC outlined its "expeditious capital (return) policy" in its investor presentation. There's a good chance that excess cash will continue to shrink with ongoing buybacks.

Separately, the company intends to cut auto-related losses to boost its group-level earnings.

It is slowing down the growth of the "Battery Electric Vehicle" or "BEV" operations to ease the cost burden. As per its corporate slides, HMC has emphasized its "reassessment of the timing to invest resources in the area of electrification".

Additional Features For New Models

As per the chart presented above, the enterprise wants to incorporate "Advanced Driver Assistance Systems" or "ADAS" functions into its future vehicles. This could allow for upward pricing adjustments.

HMC's cheapness is justified by its unimpressive mid-single digit percentage ROE. But there are hopes of multiple expansion somewhere down the road. This could materialize with financial improvement driven by repurchases and a narrower loss for automotive.

My Closing Thoughts

I have a Neutral view of Honda.

HMC's results and guidance misses were disappointing. The company will likely underperform in the foreseeable future.

But the negatives have been factored into its valuations. A re-rating of its shares over a longer time horizon is possible. This takes into account its automobile restructuring initiatives and cash distributions moves.

HMC is fairly valued, as it stands now. Its P/B discount vis-a-vis TM is aligned with the ROE difference between the two.

data.title: HubSpot's Multiple Is Still Rich, So I'm Waiting For Clearer AI Monetization
data.description:
Thesis

My research argues that HubSpot, Inc.'s (NYSE: NYSE:HUBS) latest Q2 2025 results indicate that the core business is still healthy. Sales are growing, they’re adding customers, and more of those customers are using multiple parts of the product. Breeze, its new AI platform, is a deliberate pivot to embed agents across the stack, but it’ll take time since companies have to adjust how they work and learn to trust it, so it won’t boost revenue right away. Because investors aren’t counting on near-term AI gains, the basic bet is that HubSpot will keep growing earnings by leaning on what already works (tight integration, good execution, and strong cash flow) and, over time, shift from a fast-growing disrupter into a steadier, mature software company by the time Breeze really pays off.

Why HubSpot’s Breeze May Take Until 2027 to Pay Off

Last year, HubSpot launched its AI platform called Breeze as a clear move to claim an early spot in the growing AI agent market. They timed it to come out just before Salesforce (CRM) released its own version, Agentforce, a month later. Salesforce targeted large enterprises that want heavy customization and have the resources for complex setups, while HubSpot stuck to its sweet spot: SMBs (small and mid-sized businesses) that care most about getting value quickly, integrating new tools without a lot of hassle, and paying a clear, predictable price. Breeze was designed specifically for that audience and built right into HubSpot’s existing core hubs (Marketing, Sales, and Service), where customers’ data is already stored.

However, Bank of America (BAC), in a recent report, said they don’t expect Breeze to make a meaningful impact until fiscal 2027. Essentially, for SMBs, AI agents like Breeze aren’t just a quick switch it on and go tool, and to really use them well, teams have to change how their work is organized, feel confident handing certain tasks over to the AI, and prove, consistently, that it’s actually saving them time or money before they roll it out more widely.

The outlook for Breeze is being influenced by bigger economic factors and shifts happening across the marketing industry too. Bank of America has already factored those into its forecast, and in its base case it expects Breeze’s Customer Agent feature to add about 2% extra growth by fiscal 2027.

One of those biggest industry changes happened early last year, when Gmail and Yahoo started enforcing much stricter email rules that require stronger identity checks for senders (better authentication), easier ways for people to unsubscribe, and lower thresholds for how many recipients can mark an email as spam. In the short term, these tougher rules have made life harder for marketing teams that were focused on sending as many messages as possible, they now have to clean up their mailing lists and be more precise in how they target people. But in the long run, these higher standards actually help companies that can manage campaigns across multiple channels, like email, while staying fully compliant. You could make an argument that that’s where HubSpot has an edge, because it can run all those channels from one integrated platform and over time, that could make Breeze even more valuable as part of a broader HubSpot setup.

Simultaneously, the rollout of generative AI in marketing, sales, and service is still uneven. Some teams are going all-in, producing much more work per person, while others are being careful because of risks like inaccurate outputs, brand safety concerns, and data privacy issues. Breeze’s success will depend on how well HubSpot can give teams ready-to-use AI patterns that are safe for personal data, understand business context, and follow company tone and permissions, without the need for expensive setup or consulting.

HubSpot’s Stock Lags Despite Strong Q2 Performance

Despite that structural setup, the equity market is not giving HubSpot much credit for its AI optionality right now. As of my research, the shares are down roughly 40% over six months and basically flat over twelve months at about +0.25%, lagging the S&P 500’s (SP500) roughly 22% total return.

Against that backdrop, the Q2 numbers themselves were solid and, in places, better than that. Revenue of $760.87 million grew 19.4% year over year, or 18% in constant currency, beat consensus by $20.78 million, and accelerated by about a point versus Q1.

Non-GAAP EPS of $2.19 was seven cents ahead of expectations. Operating margin printed 17%, flat year over year but up three points sequentially, which to me indicates disciplined spend while still leaning into product and go-to-market investments. Net income reached $117 million and free cash flow was $116 million, about 15% of revenue, and a $1.9 billion cash balance leaves it plenty of flexibility to keep developing products and pursuing strategic opportunities.

HubSpot’s customer numbers are helping to back up its B+ growth rating. The company now has 268,000 total customers, which is 18% higher than a year ago, and it added 9,700 new customers in the quarter, well above the top end of what management had predicted. The customer mix is also getting better in ways that boost how much each customer is likely to spend over time. About 25% of Pro+ customers are buying more core seats. Upgrades are strong too. Sales Hub seat upgrades rose 71%, and Service Hub seat upgrades surged 110%. Customers are also using more of the platform’s capabilities: 61% of new Pro+ customers signed up for multiple hubs, and 42% of all customers now use all three core hubs, both figures are up four percentage points from last year.

When it comes to AI, HubSpot’s momentum may have slowed, but customers are starting to use the tools in more meaningful ways rather than just testing them out. Customer Agent now has about 4,000 customers, with an average success rate of 55% in resolving issues, and some companies are hitting 70–80%, which has already helped deflect over one million support tickets. Prospecting Agent is being used by roughly 3,700 customers, and another 17,000 are waiting to get access. Content Agent has grown to more than 12,000 customers in just a year. HubSpot has also built connectors to ChatGPT and Claude, which around 20,000 customers have used to search through 23 million CRM records. Those AI bridges to the broader AI ecosystem are most likely already being used, so it's a pragmatic way to meet customers in the middle, so to speak, while keeping the center of gravity in HubSpot’s data environment.

Furthermore, HubSpot has been deliberately changing where its leads come from to make its marketing more stable. so now, only 10% of leads come from its blog. The other 90% now come from a blend of different channels, many of which are growing fast. Leads from YouTube have been an excellent source, those jumped 96% year over year, making it the best quarter ever for that channel. HubSpot’s LinkedIn following has grown to about two million, and Instagram now has around 600,000 followers. Newsletter-driven leads are up 50%, and the company’s podcast network is reaching roughly 5.2 million listeners.

HubSpot is also seeing steady growth with larger, more advanced customers, and its network of partners is playing a big role in that. These partners now have a hand in about 40% of HubSpot’s annual recurring revenue (ARR), and they’re also helping to directly source them. That shift is boosting both win rates and average selling prices (ASPS). Co-selling, where HubSpot and partners work together on deals, is up 29% compared to last year.

On the financial side, calculated billings, a measure of future revenue already booked, hit $814 million, which is up 20% in constant currency and 26% as reported, helped by a six-point boost from currency exchange rates.

HubSpot’s decision to raise its outlook for the year reflected that operational tone, so for all of 2025, the company now expects revenue to be between $3.08 billion and $3.088 billion, which would be 17% higher than last year. It’s also aiming for a non-GAAP operating margin of 18%, EPS in the range of $9.47 to $9.53, and about $580 million in free cash flow. The takeaway is that these targets signal that management thinks its main growth drivers: selling more seats to existing customers, getting more clients to use multiple hubs, increasing early adoption of AI tools like Breeze, and winning more business with larger companies, are strong enough to keep revenue growing in the high teens while also generating more cash.

HubSpot Guides Slower Q3 Growth, Sets Sights on 2026 Reacceleration

All of that said, before you hit the Buy button, the stock’s drawdown and the tone on the macro backdrop suggest it’s smart to stay realistic. The company still describes market conditions as uncertain and "volatile," and points out the obvious that customers are very focused on getting value for their money. The numbers back that up. Net revenue retention, a measure of how much existing customers are spending compared to before, is 103%, up just one point from the last quarter, and most of that small gain seems to come from seat upgrades tied to last year’s pricing changes, not from customers buying a lot of new features or expanding usage in other ways. Average subscription revenue per customer is about $11,300 in constant currency, which is flat compared to last year and only slightly higher when including currency effects.

These figures show that, at least for now, HubSpot’s growth is relying more on selling additional seats than on getting customers to spend more for advanced features or usage-based pricing.

HubSpot’s long-time advantage in getting leads through organic search is facing new challenges. AI-powered overviews and answer engines, the kind that give people direct answers without clicking through, are grabbing more user attention, which means fewer visits from search results. To adapt, as we just mentioned, HubSpot has been putting more effort into getting customers from other sources (i.e., YouTube), and it’s doing a good job with that. Strategically, this is the right move, but I'd argue that it also admits that the old search channel, which used to bring in a big share of leads, is under ongoing pressure. I think that the big question right now is whether these newer channels can grow large enough, and do it profitably, to match the strong economics that search used to deliver, especially after the initial easy wins from diversification are already used up.

Lastly here, for the upcoming Q3, HubSpot expects revenue of $785–$787 million, which would be about 16% growth when adjusted for currency changes. That’s a slowdown from the 18% growth it posted in Q2. Mind you, I don't consider this as a major red flag, but it does point to growth easing a bit as the year wraps up. The challenge for HubSpot now is to prove it can speed things up again in 2026. To do that, it will need to keep selling more seats, start making more money from AI tools earlier in their rollout, and take advantage of its credit system as usage grows. It will also need to expand how its AI agents are used across different types of tasks and work with partners to win more valuable deals in the larger-company market.

HubSpot Valuation Premium Narrows but Remains Elevated

In terms of valuation, its blended P/E ratio is about 51.6x, which is more than double the Information Technology sector average of 22.27 (see below).

That’s still high, but it’s way down from HubSpot’s five-year average of over 175x. Looking ahead, the forward non-GAAP P/E is around 48.67, again, over 100% higher than the sector average, but more than 70% lower than where the company used to trade.

The enterprise value-to-sales (EV/Sales) ratio shows the same pattern. On a trailing basis it’s about 8.64, and forward it’s 7.97. That’s roughly a 160–165% premium compared to the sector, but still 40–45% below the mid-teens multiples HubSpot used to get.

To me, even though during the early pandemic period it had the “category killer” valuation premium, that premium has faded, but the stock is still far from cheap.

In 2024, its P/E ratio was about 56.88, but by 2028, projections have it dropping to around 27.49, basically cutting the multiple in half over four years. If the stock price stays roughly the same, that drop would come from earnings growing enough to make the valuation more reasonable. That’s a healthier situation than the multiple shrinking because the share price falls, but it also means investors will start looking at HubSpot differently. My take is, at around 27x earnings, the market will have to shift its perception of HubSpot less as a high-flying growth stock and more like a mature software company that needs to deliver steady EPS growth and reliable cash flow.

Even after deceleration, HubSpot’s growth profile still clears the sector by a wide margin, which is the core of the premium case. Revenue grew 18.95% year over year, nearly triple the sector median of 6.67%. Forward guidance around 18.17% is again more than double the sector, though down roughly 40% from the company’s five-year average near 31.71%. This kind of slowdown is normal for a company of HubSpot’s size, but it also changes what the market will be watching most closely. Again, from here on, I'd likely to focus less on just how fast revenue is growing and more on how consistently HubSpot can keep profits strong and turn that growth into cash.

Speaking of which, looking at profitability and cash flow, HubSpot’s future outlook still looks strong. EBITDA is expected to grow about 27.08%, and EBIT is forecast to grow around 28.84%, both more than double the sector median. EPS on a diluted basis are projected to rise about 24.85%, and the long-term EPS growth rate over the next three to five years is estimated at roughly 27.71%, which is close to HubSpot’s historical pace. To me, that suggests analysts see the slower revenue growth as just a normal cooling-off period, not a sign of deeper trouble.

The cash flow data is even stronger. Levered free cash flow grew about 50.83% year over year. Forward free cash flow per share is expected to grow about 41.76%, and operating cash flow grew roughly 55.07%. This means HubSpot is pulling more cash from each new revenue dollar and that gives it room to keep spending on AI, expand its partner and app network, make selective acquisitions, and, if it wants, return money to shareholders, without risking growth.

There are a few weaker points I’m keeping an eye on. Forward return on equity (ROE) growth is expected to be about -5.59%, which is worse than the sector’s slightly negative baseline. This means HubSpot isn’t yet getting more efficient at using shareholders’ equity to generate profits. Either way, as HubSpot’s valuation multiple drops toward the high-20s P/E range, if HubSpot can improve this metric, it would help prove the company can grow and increase value per share for investors, not just grow the business overall.

Final Takeaway

Rating: Hold. I like the Q2 beat, high-teens growth, strong cash generation, and the long-run potential of Breeze embedded across HubSpot’s hubs. But with AI monetization likely immaterial until FY27, growth moderating, and the multiple still rich despite the drawdown, I’m waiting for clearer reacceleration and proof that AI can move revenue before getting more constructive on HubSpot, Inc.

data.title: DoorDash: Why Paying Up Now Could Pay Off Big Later
data.description:
Valuation has always been a divisive topic on Wall Street.

High growth tech companies often command big price tags that make value investors wince, prompting warnings of bubbles and irrational exuberance.

At the same time, history offers plenty of examples where investors were right to buy the premium, like with Amazon (AMZN) in 2012, or Netflix (NFLX) in 2015. These firms looked expensive by conventional metrics but went on to deliver incredible long-term returns for shareholders willing to look past the sticker shock.

In our view, DoorDash, Inc. (NASDAQ:DASH) may be entering a similar window of opportunity.

After a strong rally over the last year, the stock trades at a multiple that many dismiss as unsustainable - 10x sales. But that view ignores two critical realities; the firm's structural advantages & wide competitive moat, and it’s expanding role in the broader "deliver everything" economy.

While the market worries over today’s valuation, the more important question is what DASH's earnings power will look like over the next 3 - 5 years, and whether buying in now - even at a premium - could set up investors for big gains.

A few months ago, we initiated coverage on DASH with a Strong Buy rating, as one of the only bullish analysts in the room. Our contrarian opinion was in the minority, but it has been rewarded over the last quarter, with shares are up by more than 30%, while the S&P 500 (SP500) is up just over 7%:

While we expect the pace of DASH's alpha generation to slow down somewhat, the company's recent Q2 earnings release proves that the delivery juggernaut has still "got it."

With an incredible Q2 earnings beat, a re-acceleration of top line growth, and continued expansion on bottom line margins, DASH is proving itself as a "best in breed" delivery player.

Yes, shares appear expensive, and we don’t discount the fact that value investors will likely skip this one, we think the firm's incredible performance will only continue, even as the broader economy faces some turbulence.

Today, we'll highlight DoorDash’s incredible high-margin platform business model, discuss the valuation, and make the case that it’s worth paying up for DASH's stock, even after the run up in share price.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

Financials

Let’s start by taking a look at the company's most recent earnings release, as that’s what has propelled the stock in recent days.

On Wednesday, August 6th, DoorDash reported earnings-per-share of $1.51, which is a beat of $0.43, and revenue of $3.28 billion which was a beat of roughly $120 million:

While DASH's results continue the mostly positive track record of beating analyst estimates, what really grabbed our attention here was the scale of the earnings beat, along with the fact that top line sales growth accelerated to nearly 25% YoY.

This shows that DASH has been executing and growing, even in a period where many expected deliveries would be cut out of stressed consumer budgets.

At the same time, on the bottom line, DASH turned in its highest quarter ever in terms of net margins, posting a figure of roughly 8.7%:

When zoomed out, this continues the company's streak of improving bottom line margins will continuing to grow top line sales:

In our view, this is one of the best combinations you can have in a growth story - it proves that the company has a considerable market opportunity, and it also shows that management has found a way to "unlock" operating leverage within a business. With gross margins of roughly 51% in Q2 of this year, we believe that over the next 2 to 3 years, DoorDash's net margins could grow to as much as 20%.

At present, analysts are currently estimating that 2028 revenue - roughly 3 years away - will come in in about $22 billion:

If DoorDash can achieve this, the company has the potential to produce $4.5 to $5 billion in net income within a very short space of time.

We’ve seen this time and time again with high growth companies, like Uber (UBER), that have strong revenue growth flywheels, but have purposely held off on earning a profit in order to reinvest. Now, as these companies mature publicly, the bottom line has become more important, and we've found that investing at this profitability inflection point has proven a successful strategy.

On a granular basis, in the most recent quarter, DoorDash management highlighted the fact that the firm's ad business has now grown to roughly $1 billion in ARR, which is a tremendous accomplishment and should boost margins considerably. It also shows how powerful the DoorDash platform is becoming, especially in terms of attracting consumer attention and convenience dollars.

More broadly, our investment thesis rests on the idea that management is focused on long-term margin dollars, not short-term unit economics:

[O]ur investment philosophy has always been one of maximizing long-term margin dollars, not short-term unit margins

To us, this builds the expectation that there’s incredible scalability in terms of margins here, but we expect that management will allow this number to "glide" higher at a controlled rate, while still investing in necessary R&D and OpEx.

All told, we think DoorDash sits in an incredible position, as continued vertical & horizontal expansion in several major markets should power long-term growth. At the same time, management's considered budgeting should offer healthy long-term margin gains.

Combined, we’re expecting strong organic performance for DoorDash through the end of the decade in terms of both top line sales and bottom-line EPS increases.

Valuation

Much of the negative analysis we’ve read on DASH has to do with the stock's expanded valuation. It’s true. The stock looks expensive, trading it nearly 10x trailing revenues and more than 100x trailing earnings. The market, more broadly, also isn't' cheap - especially as the S&P 500 inches closer to a 25x earnings multiple:

It’s easy to point out DoorDash and its current multiple as part of the market's overall frothiness.

That said, if you look at DASH's growth trajectory, then we find the valuation entirely stomach-able. If sales grow to $22 billion by the end of 2028, then the stock will only be trading at roughly 5x sales:

Plus, in the event that earnings grow at the rate we projected earlier - which appears to be a base case - then you could be looking at a company with $110 in billion market cap, producing roughly $5 billion a year in net profit. This implies a 2028 valuation of roughly 22x earnings, which appears much more reasonable (and even cheap, nominally) versus where the market sits today.

When compared versus peers in the delivery game, DASH also trades at a favorable price point.

DASH enjoys stronger top line growth than peers Uber and Grab (GRAB), while sporting higher gross margins:

This contrasts with the firm's valuation, which comparably priced over time:

When viewed nominally, vs. peers, or even vs. itself, we think DASH appears priced at a premium, but one we're willing to spend up for:

Thus, when you add it up, we think DASH's strong organic performance, excellent Q2 results, and reasonable - if slightly premium - valuation combine for us to re-iterate our Strong Buy rating.

Risks

Of course, while we think the valuation appears reasonable given the long term growth prospects, we acknowledge that it presents the core risk vector for the stock - at least over the short and medium term.

During market downturns, stocks with high multiples tend to get trimmed first, and in the 2021 boom / 2022 bust cycle, many high multiple stocks, including DASH, saw their multiples cut from 20x+ sales to less than 3x sales. This represents serious margin compression, and a real risk to shareholders.

Of course, things are different right now.

DASH's free cash flow is pumping (aside from the huge Deliveroo investment), and we don't expect a monetary re-run of the inflation we saw in 2022. That said, there's always a risk that DASH could command a lower multiple in the event of a steep market contraction, and as the S&P 500 gets more and more expensive, this is an outcome we're more and more concerned about.

This doesn't represent an organic risk to the business, but its' important for shareholders to know.

Summary

Overall, though, we expect that DASH will continue to appreciate in the short, medium, and long term, especially on the back of its' new all-time highs streak.

With surging organic numbers, a reasonable - if somewhat premium - multiple, and the company's strong unit economics, we're finding it hard to rate DASH anything other than a Strong Buy following the Q2 report.

Cheers~

data.title: Chemours: Solid Q2 And Legal Progress Are Bullish (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Chemours (NYSE:CC) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing over a third of their value. It has been a challenging year for the chemical industry more broadly given lackluster demand, and Chemours has even had to cut its dividend to preserve cash flow. We are seeing some progress in both financial results and reducing legal overhangs. I last covered shares in May when I rated CC a hold as I felt the dividend cut could represent the worst of the news flow. Since then, shares have bounced 16%, in-line with the market’s 13% recovery and consistent with my rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit CC. I am more bullish.

In the company’s second quarter, Chemours earned $0.58, which beat estimates by $0.12 as revenue grew by 4% to $1.6 billion. Overall, the company generated $253 million in EBITDA, up from $207 million a year ago. Pricing was a net $14 million benefit, primarily in Opteon, offset by lower titanium pricing. Volumes were a $34 million tailwind, again driven by Opteon. I was encouraged to see the company report $50 million of free cash flow, consistent with my view that we would see this metric rebound after a weak Q1. Still, CC has -$146 million of free cash flow year-to-date due to $156 million of working capital headwinds.

Looking at segment results, Thermal & Specialized Solutions grew sales by 15% to $597 million as Opteon refrigerant sales surged by 65%. Opteon now accounts for over 60% of the business, up from 44% last year. Adjusted EBITDA surged by $47 million to $207 million. Pricing was up 4% from last year.

Given regulatory changes that have restricted new sales of Freon, Chemours’ Opteon refrigerant has boomed, given its lower environmental impact. However, this has been a self-cannibalization to a degree. This is still preferable to losing the business to another competitor, but it is important to keep in mind that Opteon’s success comes at Freon’s expense to an extent. Still, Opteon sales were up nearly $150 million to $375 million while Freon sales were down by $50 million to $123 million. So while Freon is detractor, Chemours is proving to be a net-winner from this transition.

Titanium Technologies saw revenue fall by 3% to $657 million, resulting in a 43% drop in EBITDA to $47 million as pricing continues to come under pressure even as input costs rise. It is worth emphasizing that while this unit generates $60 million more in sales than TSS, it generates $160 million less in EBITDA, given its more commodity nature. Pricing was down 4% while volumes were flat with the US and Europe up while the rest of the world saw declines.

Results were impacted by rail service disruptions, which forced the company to buy higher priced feedstock, a $15 million headwind to profits. In practice, Chemours made the decision to still fulfill orders despite supply chain outages to underlines its reliability as a partner while sacrificing margin. As these disruptions resolve, I expect to see some sequential recovery in margins, most likely in the fourth quarter.

Finally, Advanced Performance Materials grew EBITDA by 11% to $50 million on flat revenue of $346 million. Pricing was up by 6% due to a mix shift towards higher value-add products while volumes decline by 6%, given weakness in the industrial economy. I expect this unit to continue to report sluggish revenue growth.

Separate from its financial results, Chemours also reached a deal with New Jersey to reserve all environmental litigation. Chemours is one of the companies that has been weighed down by claims related to PFAS, or “forever chemicals.” This settlement in resulted in a $257 million charge. I view this as a net positive as Chemours continues to clear out the environmental overhang that has weighed on the stock since being spun out by DuPont (DD).

As explained on the earnings call, under the New Jersey deal, there will be payments over 25 years with a present value of $250 million. By selling its insurance proceeds for $150 million and releasing $50 million from its escrow account, its obligations under the agreement are covered through 2030. That leaves Chemours with $80 million of potential further cash needs, barring further insurance proceeds. While it has $750 million of potential insurance claims, it is highly confident in the receipt of $300 million, suggesting $150 million of future proceeds. There have been $2.6 billion in settlements so far.

Alongside results, management reaffirmed a full-year outlook of $775-$825 million. It expects $175-195 million in Q3 EBITDA, which reflects $35 million of Q3 disruptions in APM and TT, which also impacted Q2 results and should be resolved by the fourth quarter.

Chemours continues to have a stretched balance sheet with $502 million of unrestricted cash and $3.7 billion of net debt. That leaves it with 4.7x leverage. Now, we should see free cash flow improve meaningfully in the second half the of year as working capital draws reverse. As such, I see its net debt position dropping by at least $400 million by year end, leaving CC at closer to 4x levered. I still would like to see leverage drop below 3.5x, and I expect CC to maintain its more modest 2.9% dividend yield for the foreseeable future as leverage is unlikely to reach this point before the end of 2026 to mid 2027.

Back in May, I was targeting $275-300 million of full year free cash flow, which would have left it with $3.2 billion of year end debt. I now see some risk of free cash flow coming in slightly below the $275 million level, given a slower recovery in working capital. I still expect CC to generate about $800 million of EBITDA, given growing momentum in its Opteon product line.

Shares are trading about 6.4x EV/EBITDA (using anticipated year end debt balances) and with a ~ 14-15% free cash flow yield. This elevated free cash flow yield speaks to the need to retain cash flow to reduce debt as well as lingering PFAS litigation overhang. I have seen potential upside as these claims get resolved to a 7x EV/EBITDA, or about $16, which would leave shares with a ~12% free cash flow yield.

Given the NJ settlement and ability to structure it with no cash outflows before 2030, I see CC better positioned to expand its multiple, especially with Opteon growth and some potential incremental cooling demand from data centers. As such, I see shares as being able to move towards $16 this year. I am upgrading shares to a “buy,” as I see the recovery poised to continue, enabling future outperformance.

data.title: Maui Land & Pineapple Company: Development Plays Could Be In Again If Rates Fall
data.description:
Maui Land & Pineapple Company, Inc. (NYSE:MLP) is a company with possibly pretty significant latent value from development opportunities on its properties, particularly the residential assets which are located in the area of the Maui Kapalua resort. The resort is composed of existing residential properties but also a Ritz-Carlton hotel, and amenities like the Plantation Golf Course (home to the first PGA tournament each year), the latter operated by MLP and present as one of the businesses' three segments. As there hasn't been a lot of activity on the development front lately that would be appearing on the income statement, due to the stock's small and idiosyncratic profile together with development market conditions, most of the revenue has been coming from the leasing business, where they get income from leases of properties to commercial customers. Even just the value of residential single-family homes that they are planning on developing in the Kapalua Mauka project could be estimated at around 1.5x the current market cap. With the company currently being pretty low debt, that is already a considerable margin of safety. MLP could also be a timely play given the possible change in direction of rate policies, reflected in the downward shift of the yield curve. There may indeed be something to MLP and it will be worth following to see the progress in developing their assets.

Discussing results and assets

MLP is a real estate business that does land development and has a leasing portfolio, in addition to income from a golfing club and other amenities, all related to the Kapalua Resort in West Maui, which is home to a lot of residences and also commercial activities like hotels and restaurants. In other words, it's a nice place in Hawaii, and is tourism dependent, and therefore dependent primarily on the state of the mainland West Coast economy, or the broader US economy if aligned. The main macro consideration that we care about is the interest rate situation. It is well known that the current interest rate situation has been a generally bad condition for housing starts and has made the development markets, which are leverage dependent both on the supply and the demand side, rather gelid. This has been the case across mainland America as well. With reopened travel, this is the outstanding condition for MLP to develop into a willing market.

Starting with the development portfolio, these are their land holdings as of the FY.

They are working on getting the right to develop these lands for various residential and commercial uses. Kapalua Makai is a 37 acre project, and could open up a lot of residences. The Kapalua Central resort is comprised of 46 acres, which will be home to 196 residential units among other real estate. Kapalua Mauka has a remaining 930 acre area owned by MLP, and already has land entitlements to produce 639 single family homes, but also other recreation space and amenities. This is the most actual at the moment with pre-development plans underway, but we're going with the residential mixed use figure of 866 for the calculation. Taking a very crude approach, based on Redfin data we might be looking at around $700 million in market value for the real estate - around $35 per share on a ~$17 per share market cap. Take it with a grain of salt. With taxes we are looking at a ~$28 per share or so, but the calculation is still quite crude, and we also don't expect it to be reflected into valuation until more news comes for the state of the developments.

In terms of the latest results, they are working on relief housing, and pricing those contracted services at zero margin. So there is growth in land development and sales topline. We understand this is related to the fires in Hawaii. While a nice initiative and good for the community, it is empty calories for shareholders and doesn't either help or weaken profits in and of itself. As of the FY figures, where the above is from the May 8-K, land development sales are still a negligible as a proportion of revenue. Leasing is the main income for the moment. NOI on the leasing is around $1.9 million quarterly according to this latest data, where growing occupancy rates have considerably increased lease revenues particularly on agricultural properties, including for new agave planting, all helping to utilise underutilised property. Using a 5% cap rate benchmark and therefore multiplying the $1.9*4 million for annualised figures by 20x we get a valuation of around $160 million for the leases in terms of market value. Market cap is $335 million. Again, this adds margin of safety.

Bottom Line

We think the company is cheap. There is a lot of embedded value in just the residential land which could be made into something valuable. There has even been success in monetising agricultural land on the lease side, which signals there may be embedded value in the agricultural portfolio as well. The moment isn't bad for real estate and development ideas either. There is dissent at the Fed. Soon after some signaling by the Fed that initially seemed a little inconsistent with inflation trends about cutting interest rates, the jobs report came out that signaled that maybe the growth mandate must come into play. Markets are now taking the chances of rate cuts seriously again. In the short and medium term the yield curve is shifted down meaningfully since a month ago by around 17-18 bps depending on the horizon. This could unfreeze the situation a bit in development and in multiples on real estate more broadly, though the MLP situation is quite idiosyncratic and linked to Maui and the Kapalua resort, and the progress that can be made with local government to move along the development. However, with embedded value at around 2x the market cap and with limited debt even including pension liabilities, there is an upside on this rather under the radar and apparently forgotten pick. There will be a point where more leveraged-market real estate plays like MLP and other development plays come back into favour. It's good to know that MLP might be one with meaningful upside, so we will keep our eye on it. But we want to see some news first.
